section.title: Seek the LORD and Live
section.sectionRationale: The heart of the book. Amos raises a funeral song over Israel while she still lives, then presses the great gospel appeal, seek Me and live, three times over. He exposes the corruption of the courts, shatters the false hope of the Day of the LORD, and delivers God's stunning rejection of worship without justice, crowned by the book's most famous verse: let justice roll on like a river.
section.thesis: Israel is already as good as dead, yet the door of life stands open in the call to seek the LORD; and the God who is worshiped with songs He hates will accept nothing less than justice rolling like a river and righteousness like a never-failing stream.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the funeral lament; the threefold call to seek and live; the corruption of justice under the Creator's eye; the plea to seek good; the darkness of the Day of the LORD; and worship rejected in favor of justice.

units.label: A funeral song for the living

verses.text: Hear this word, O house of Israel, this lamentation I take up against you:
“Fallen is Virgin Israel, never to rise again. She lies abandoned on her land, with no one to raise her up.”

This is what the Lord GOD says: “The city that marches out a thousand strong will have but a hundred left, and the one that marches out a hundred strong will have but ten left in the house of Israel.”

units.label: Seek Me and live
units.note: not Bethel, not Gilgal, but the LORD

verses.text: For this is what the LORD says to the house of Israel: “{{darash|Seek}} Me and live!
Do not seek Bethel or go to Gilgal; do not journey to Beersheba, for Gilgal will surely go into exile, and Bethel will come to nothing.
Seek the LORD and live, or He will sweep like fire through the house of Joseph; it will devour everything, with no one at Bethel to extinguish it.

units.label: Justice turned to wormwood
units.note: the Maker of the Pleiades sees

verses.text: There are those who turn {{mishpat|justice}} into wormwood and cast {{tsedaqah|righteousness}} to the ground.
He who made the Pleiades and Orion, who turns darkness into dawn and darkens day into night, who summons the waters of the sea and pours them over the face of the earth, the LORD is His name,
He flashes destruction on the strong, so that fury comes upon the stronghold.
There are those who hate the one who reproves in the gate and despise him who speaks with integrity.

Therefore, because you trample on the poor and exact from him a tax of grain, you will never live in the stone houses you have built; you will never drink the wine from the lush vineyards you have planted.
For I know that your transgressions are many and your sins are numerous. You oppress the righteous by taking bribes; you deprive the poor of justice in the gate.
Therefore, the prudent keep silent in such times, for the days are evil.

units.label: Seek good and live
units.note: hate evil, love good, establish justice

verses.text: Seek good, not evil, so that you may live. And the LORD, the God of Hosts, will be with you, as you have claimed.
Hate evil and love good; establish justice in the gate. Perhaps the LORD, the God of Hosts, will be gracious to the {{sheerit|remnant}} of Joseph.”

units.label: The Day of the LORD is darkness

verses.text: Therefore this is what the LORD, the God of Hosts, the Lord, says: “There will be wailing in all the public squares and cries of ‘Alas! Alas!’ in all the streets. The farmer will be summoned to mourn, and the mourners to wail.
There will be wailing in all the vineyards, for I will pass through your midst,” says the LORD.
Woe to you who long for the {{yom|Day of the LORD}}! What will the Day of the LORD be for you? It will be darkness and not light.
It will be like a man who flees from a lion, only to encounter a bear, or who enters his house and rests his hand against the wall, only to be bitten by a snake.
Will not the Day of the LORD be darkness and not light, even gloom with no brightness in it?

units.label: Let justice roll
units.note: worship God hates, justice God wants

verses.text: “I hate, I despise your feasts! I cannot stand the stench of your solemn assemblies.
Even though you offer Me burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them; for your peace offerings of fattened cattle I will have no regard.
Take away from Me the noise of your songs! I will not listen to the music of your harps.
But let {{mishpat|justice}} roll on like a river, and {{tsedaqah|righteousness}} like an ever-flowing stream.
Did you bring Me sacrifices and offerings forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?
You have taken along Sakkuth your king and Kaiwan your star god, the idols you made for yourselves.
Therefore I will send you into exile beyond Damascus,” says the LORD, whose name is the God of Hosts.

darash.gloss: To seek, search out, resort to, inquire of. In this chapter it is God's central appeal, seek Me and live, and it is set against seeking the shrines of Bethel and Gilgal. To darash the LORD is to turn one's whole life toward Him for help and direction, and Amos insists that this seeking, not busy pilgrimage to false altars, is the path to life.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of life especially in the courts and toward the vulnerable. Israel turned mishpat into bitter wormwood, corrupting the gate where cases were heard. God's demand in verse 24 is that mishpat roll on like a river: not an occasional trickle of fairness but a flood that never dries up, carrying life to everyone in its path.

tsedaqah.gloss: Righteousness, right relationship expressed in right conduct toward God and neighbor. Paired with mishpat, it describes a community shaped by God's own character. Israel cast tsedaqah to the ground; God wants it to flow like an ever-flowing stream, a wadi that, unlike the seasonal streams of the land, never runs dry even in the heat of summer.

sheerit.gloss: Remnant, remainder, what survives after disaster. Amos holds out a fragile hope: perhaps the LORD will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph. Judgment will fall, yet God may preserve a surviving few. This slender thread of the remnant runs through the prophets and is finally secured in Christ, in whom a people is saved out of the wreckage.

yom.gloss: The Day of the LORD, the day God decisively acts to judge His enemies and vindicate His people. Israel eagerly awaited it, assuming they were on the right side. Amos shatters that presumption: for an unrepentant covenant people the Day will be darkness, not light, like fleeing a lion straight into a bear. The Day is only good news for those who truly belong to God.

basic.context: This central chapter mixes lament, appeal, and warning. Amos sings a funeral song over Israel as if she has already fallen, yet three times he pleads, seek the LORD and live. He condemns bribed judges who crush the poor at the city gate. He warns those who long for the Day of the LORD that it will be darkness, not light. And he delivers God's shocking verdict: God hates their festivals and songs and wants instead justice rolling like a river and righteousness like a never-drying stream.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter holds together severe judgment and genuine invitation. Israel is as good as dead, yet the offer of life is real: seek God, not the shrines. The famous line about justice rolling down is not a rejection of worship as such, but of worship that coexists with injustice; God wants worshipers whose lives are just.
basic.originalLanguages: The pivotal verb is darash, to seek: seek Me and live. Israel had turned mishpat, justice, into bitter wormwood and cast tsedaqah, righteousness, to the ground; God wants both to flow unstoppably. He offers grace to a possible sheerit, a remnant. And He redefines the yom YHWH, the Day of the LORD, as darkness for the unrepentant.
basic.typology: The call to seek the LORD and live points to Christ, in whom God is truly found and life is truly given. The remnant God may spare finds its fulfillment in the people saved in Christ. And the demand for justice like a river anticipates the King whose kingdom is everlasting righteousness.
basic.christ: Seek Me and live is answered fully in Jesus, who says whoever comes to Him He will never cast out, and who is Himself the life. The Day of the LORD, darkness for the guilty, fell in its fullness on Christ at the cross, where the sun went dark at noon, so that for those in Him the Day becomes light. And the justice and righteousness God demands are both fulfilled and supplied in Christ.
basic.god: God is at once the righteous Judge who sings a funeral song over sin and the gracious Savior who pleads seek Me and live. He is the Creator who made the stars and rules the seas, so His moral demands carry cosmic weight. And He cares so deeply about justice that He will not accept worship offered by hands that oppress the poor.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 6:33 tells us to seek first God's kingdom and righteousness. Isaiah 1 shows God rejecting worship joined to injustice. Micah 6:8 sums up what God requires. Acts 7 cites Amos 5:25-27 in Stephen's sermon.
basic.oneStory: At the center of Amos stands both a funeral and an invitation: death for the impenitent, life for those who seek the LORD. The plea seek Me and live runs through Scripture to Christ, in whom God is found, the Day of darkness is borne, and justice at last rolls on like an unfailing river.

context.literary: Chapter 5 is a carefully built chiasm, folding inward toward its center. It opens and closes with lament and judgment, and moves through the repeated summons to seek. Two creation hymns punctuate it, verses 8-9, grounding the call in God's cosmic power. The rhetorical high points are the threefold seek and live, the woe on those who long for the Day of the LORD, and the thunderous let justice roll on like a river.
context.historical: The gate was the place where legal cases were heard and the vulnerable should have found protection; instead judges took bribes and silenced honest witnesses. Bethel, Gilgal, and Beersheba were popular pilgrimage shrines. The Day of the LORD was a cherished national hope, expecting God to crush Israel's enemies; Amos turns it against a people who are themselves God's enemies. Sakkuth and Kaiwan were Assyrian-Babylonian astral deities, showing idolatry mixed into Israel's worship.
context.canonical: Amos 5:24 became a watchword for biblical justice, quoted across the centuries. Amos 5:18-20 is the earliest clear reference to the Day of the LORD as a day of darkness for the presumptuous, a theme developed by Joel, Zephaniah, and the New Testament. Stephen quotes Amos 5:25-27 in Acts 7:42-43 to indict Israel's long history of idolatry. Jesus' seek first the kingdom and its righteousness draws on the same seeking-and-life logic.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lament over a people as good as dead, to press upon them the real and open offer of life in seeking the LORD, to expose corrupt justice and empty worship, and to strip away the false comfort of a Day of the LORD they were not ready for.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter both describes coming judgment and prescribes the way of life: seek the LORD, seek good, establish justice. Amos 5:24 is a prescriptive demand for enduring justice and righteousness; the funeral song is descriptive of what impenitence brings.

debates.question: Does Amos 5:21-24 reject sacrificial worship itself, or worship divorced from justice?

views.label: A rejection of empty ritual, not of worship
views.case: God instituted the very feasts and offerings He here rejects; the target is worship offered by oppressors, hollow ceremony that masks injustice, so He calls for justice to accompany, not replace, true worship.

views.label: A radical prioritizing of justice
views.case: The sweeping language, I hate, I despise, take away the noise of your songs, shows that where justice is absent, God counts even prescribed worship as offensive noise; justice is not optional decoration but the test of real devotion.

debates.bearing: Both agree God will not be worshiped by unjust hands. Worship and justice are inseparable: praise from a life that tramples the poor is a stench to God, while true worship overflows into justice rolling like a river.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on the verb darash, to seek, resort to, inquire of, sounded three times: seek Me, do not seek Bethel, seek the LORD and live. Israel had done the opposite with the two great covenant words, turning mishpat, justice, into laanah, wormwood, a bitter, poisonous plant, and casting tsedaqah, righteousness, to the ground. God's answer in verse 24 is that mishpat should roll like a perennial river and tsedaqah like a nachal ethan, an ever-flowing wadi that never dries even in the drought of summer, a stunning image in a land of seasonal streams. Amos offers grace to a possible she'erit, remnant, and overturns the popular hope of the yom YHWH, the Day of the LORD, declaring it darkness and not light for a people who are themselves in the wrong.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom God is found and life is given; whoever comes to Him He will never cast out, John 6:37, and He is the life, John 14:6.

typology.type: The Day of the LORD as darkness, 5:18-20
typology.fulfillment: The darkness that fell at the cross, where Christ bore the Day of judgment so that for His people it becomes light, Matthew 27:45, 1 Thessalonians 5:2-10.

typology.type: The remnant of Joseph God may spare, 5:15
typology.fulfillment: The remnant chosen by grace and secured in Christ, Romans 11:5, saved out of the coming judgment.

study.christ: The chapter's threefold plea, seek Me and live, is answered in Jesus, who is Himself the life and who promises never to cast out anyone who comes to Him. The Day of the LORD that Amos redefines as darkness fell in full at Calvary, when the sun went dark at noon and Christ absorbed the day of judgment; because He bore that darkness, the Day dawns as light for all who are His, 1 Thessalonians 5:5. And the justice and righteousness God demands, which Israel could not produce, are both accomplished by Christ, who is our righteousness, and poured out through His Spirit so that His people become, at last, a stream of justice in the world.
study.god: God is the grieving Judge who takes up a funeral song over His own people, and in the same breath the gracious Savior who pleads seek Me and live. He is the Creator of the Pleiades and Orion, Lord of dawn and sea, so His claims are not local rules but the will of the One who made all things. He is scandalously honest about worship: songs and sacrifices from oppressors are a stench He cannot bear. And His justice is not a trickle but a flood; He will have righteousness that never runs dry.

crossReferences.note: Seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, the New Testament echo of seek Me and live.
God rejects sacrifices from unjust hands and calls instead for justice and defense of the vulnerable, parallel to Amos 5:21-24.
The Day of the LORD comes as darkness on the presumptuous but as light on the children of the day, developing Amos 5:18-20.
Stephen quotes Amos 5:25-27 to expose Israel's long history of idolatry, culminating in rejecting the Righteous One.

study.oneStory: The center of Amos is a funeral and an invitation held together: judgment for the impenitent, life for those who seek the LORD. That plea runs the length of Scripture and lands on Jesus, who is the life we seek, who bore the Day of darkness in our place, and who pours out the justice and righteousness God has always demanded, until they roll on like a river that never fails.

quiz.q: How can Amos sing a funeral song over Israel and still say seek Me and live?

options.text: Israel is as good as dead under judgment, yet God genuinely holds open the door of life to those who turn and seek Him.
options.note: Judgment and real invitation stand together in the chapter.

options.text: The funeral song is only symbolic and there is no real danger.
options.note: The threat of death is real; so is the offer of life.

options.text: God laments the coming ruin but still offers a way to live.
options.note: Right about both; the sharp point is that Israel is already as good as dead, yet life is offered.

options.text: God changed His mind and cancelled the judgment.
options.note: The judgment stands; life comes through seeking Him, not through its cancellation.

quiz.q: What does God mean by let justice roll on like a river and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream?

options.text: He wants constant, life-giving justice and righteousness that never dry up, not an occasional trickle of fairness.
options.note: The ever-flowing stream never fails, even in summer drought.

options.text: He wants Israel to build irrigation for their crops.
options.note: The river is a metaphor for enduring justice and righteousness.

options.text: He wants justice and righteousness to flow through the community.
options.note: Right, and the image stresses that it must be constant and never-failing.

options.text: He wants occasional acts of charity when convenient.
options.note: The picture is a flood that never dries up, not occasional charity.

quiz.q: Why does God say He hates Israel's feasts and songs?

options.text: Because worship offered by people who oppress the poor is offensive to Him; He will not be worshiped by unjust hands.
options.note: Worship and justice are inseparable in God's sight.

options.text: Because God dislikes music and festivals in general.
options.note: God instituted these feasts; the problem is injustice, not worship itself.

options.text: Because their worship was disconnected from just living.
options.note: Right, and God's language shows how deeply such hollow worship offends Him.

options.text: Because they used the wrong instruments.
options.note: The issue is the injustice behind the worship, not the instruments.

quiz.q: Why does Amos say the Day of the LORD will be darkness, not light?

options.text: Israel presumed the Day would crush their enemies, but as God's unrepentant people they themselves face judgment.
options.note: The Day is only good news for those truly on God's side.

options.text: Because it will literally be a cloudy day.
options.note: Darkness here means judgment, not weather.

options.text: Because they were wrongly confident they were safe on that Day.
options.note: Right about false confidence; specifically their own sin makes the Day dark for them.

options.text: Because the Day of the LORD is always bad for everyone.
options.note: For the faithful the Day is light; it is darkness for the impenitent.

quiz.q: How does the Day of the LORD as darkness point to Christ?

options.text: The darkness of judgment fell on Jesus at the cross, so that for His people the Day becomes light.
options.note: The sun darkened at noon as Christ bore the day of judgment.

options.text: It simply warns us to be ready for the end of the world.
options.note: True in part, but Scripture ties the darkness specifically to the cross.

options.text: It shows Jesus also spoke about a coming day of judgment.
options.note: Right that Jesus taught it, but the deeper link is that He bore the darkness for us.

options.text: It has no relationship to Jesus.
options.note: The New Testament connects the Day and the darkness directly to Christ.

questions: Three times God pleads, seek Me and live. What are the Bethels and Gilgals, the substitutes for God, that you find yourself seeking instead of Him?
God will not accept worship from hands that oppress the poor. Is there any injustice in your life or dealings that makes your worship hollow?
Justice was meant to roll like a river that never dries up. Where is God calling you to move from occasional fairness to steady, flowing righteousness?
Israel wrongly assumed the Day of the LORD would be light for them. Where might you be presuming on God's favor without truly seeking Him?
Jesus bore the day of darkness so it could become light for His people. How does that turn your dread of judgment into confidence, and gratitude?

translationNotes.note: Bethel will come to nothing renders a Hebrew wordplay: Bethel, house of God, becomes Beth-aven, house of nothing or house of wickedness, a derogatory nickname also used in Hosea.
Ever-flowing stream translates nachal ethan, a wadi that keeps running even in the dry season, unlike the many seasonal streams of the land; the image stresses justice that never fails.
Sakkuth and Kaiwan are astral deities; the Septuagint renders them differently, and Stephen follows that reading when he cites the passage in Acts 7:42-43.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Amos 5:1-27. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; footnote markers omitted; trailing em dash of verse 8 rendered as a comma per house style. Tokens for justice and righteousness placed at both verse 7 and the climactic verse 24.
